one_liner: A 62-year-old man with a history of chronic hepatitis C and cirrhosis presents with 3 months of right upper quadrant pain, 20-pound unintentional weight loss, and new-onset abdominal distension.

stage2.hpi_extension: He describes a dull, constant ache in the right upper quadrant that has progressively worsened. Over the past month he has noticed his pants fitting more loosely around his waist but a bulging abdomen, early satiety, and worsening fatigue. He also reports one episode of coffee-ground emesis last week. No fever or chills. He was diagnosed with hepatitis C 15 years ago after a blood transfusion and has been followed intermittently.
stage2.pmh: Chronic hepatitis C (genotype 1a, untreated), compensated cirrhosis (Child-Pugh A) followed every 2 years, hypertension
stage2.social_history: Former IV drug user (quit 25 years ago). Drinks a 6-pack of beer on weekends. Former construction worker.
stage2.physical_exam: General: thin man with temporal wasting and mild scleral icterus. Skin: several spider angiomas on the upper chest, palmar erythema, and scattered ecchymoses. Chest: gynecomastia. Abdomen: distended with a positive fluid wave and shifting dullness (ascites). Tender, firm, nodular hepatomegaly extending 5 cm below the right costal margin with an audible bruit over the liver. Splenomegaly. Extremities: bilateral lower extremity pitting edema. Neuro: mild asterixis.

lab_results.result: Total bilirubin 3.1 mg/dL, AST 142, ALT 98, alkaline phosphatase 310, albumin 2.6 g/dL, INR 1.5, platelets 78,000/uL, creatinine 1.1. Sodium 131.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Hypoalbuminemia, elevated INR, thrombocytopenia, and hyponatremia reflect underlying cirrhosis with decompensation. AST > ALT fits chronic liver disease. Rising bilirubin suggests worsening hepatic synthetic function.

lab_results.result: Alpha-fetoprotein (AFP): 2,840 ng/mL (reference <10). Anti-HCV positive; HCV RNA detectable. HBsAg negative. CA 19-9 mildly elevated.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Markedly elevated AFP (>400-500) in a cirrhotic patient with a liver mass is essentially diagnostic of hepatocellular carcinoma. Persistent HCV viremia identifies the underlying risk factor.

lab_results.result: Multiphase contrast MRI of the liver: 6 cm arterial phase-enhancing lesion in segment VIII with portal venous phase washout and delayed capsule enhancement (LI-RADS 5). Background nodular cirrhotic liver with splenomegaly, recanalized paraumbilical vein, and moderate ascites. Tumor thrombus in the right portal vein.
lab_results.clinical_significance: The classic triad of arterial hyperenhancement + washout + capsule on multiphase imaging in a cirrhotic liver is pathognomonic for HCC (LI-RADS 5, no biopsy required). Portal vein invasion indicates advanced stage (BCLC C) and worsens prognosis significantly.

stage5.final_clue: The patient is staged as BCLC C (advanced HCC with portal vein invasion and preserved performance status). He is not a transplant candidate due to tumor size and vascular invasion (exceeds Milan criteria). He is started on sorafenib, a multikinase inhibitor, for palliative systemic therapy.

debrief.classic_presentation: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) classically arises in patients with cirrhosis from any cause and presents with worsening right upper quadrant pain, unintentional weight loss, early satiety, and sudden decompensation of previously stable cirrhosis (new or worsening ascites, jaundice, encephalopathy, or variceal bleeding). Physical findings include a firm, nodular, often tender hepatomegaly and sometimes a hepatic bruit. Paraneoplastic findings include polycythemia (ectopic erythropoietin), hypoglycemia (ectopic insulin-like growth factor), and hypercalcemia.
debrief.pathophysiology: HCC arises in a background of chronic hepatocellular injury and regeneration. Major risk factors: chronic HBV (direct oncogenic effect - can cause HCC even without cirrhosis), chronic HCV, alcoholic liver disease, non-alcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH), hereditary hemochromatosis, alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency, Wilson disease, and aflatoxin (Aspergillus flavus contamination of peanuts/grains - p53 mutation at codon 249). Tumors spread hematogenously and commonly invade the portal vein, hepatic veins, and IVC. Metastases most often go to lungs.
debrief.treatment_overview: Surveillance: every-6-month liver ultrasound +/- AFP in at-risk patients (cirrhosis, chronic HBV). Treatment depends on BCLC stage: Milan criteria (1 tumor <=5 cm, or up to 3 tumors each <=3 cm, no vascular invasion, no mets) define transplant candidates with the best survival. Early stage: resection, ablation (RFA), or transplant. Intermediate: transarterial chemoembolization (TACE). Advanced (vascular invasion or extrahepatic spread): sorafenib or lenvatinib (multikinase inhibitors). Terminal: palliative care. Overall prognosis is poor; prevention (HBV vaccination, HCV treatment) is key.
high_yield_pearls: HCC risk factors: chronic HBV, HCV, alcoholic cirrhosis, NASH, hemochromatosis, alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency, Wilson disease, and aflatoxin (causes characteristic p53 codon 249 mutation).
AFP is the classic tumor marker for HCC; surveillance is liver ultrasound every 6 months in cirrhotic patients.
A liver mass with arterial enhancement + portal venous washout in a cirrhotic liver is diagnostic for HCC - biopsy is not required.
Hepatitis B virus can cause HCC without cirrhosis because HBV DNA integrates into the host genome and has direct oncogenic effects.
Milan criteria (single tumor <=5 cm OR up to 3 tumors each <=3 cm, no vascular invasion/mets) identify HCC patients eligible for liver transplantation with excellent 5-year survival.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) - Gastrointestinal chapter, 'Hepatocellular carcinoma / hepatoma' section.
First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) - Gastrointestinal chapter, 'Cirrhosis and portal hypertension' section.
